When the Same Insult Targets a Woman or a Man: CHB-style Draft

Xuan Bao

Abstract

Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used to rate hostile content. Human–LLM agreement in Chinese gendered attack ratings remains unclear because naturally occurring text entangles target gender with attack content and context. This study tested whether LLMs and Chinese human raters perceive the same hostile content as more aggressive in female-targeted versus male-targeted versions, and whether this target-gender gap depends on attack domain and response scale. We constructed 371 Chinese gender-reversal minimal pairs of attack-laden sentences and collected ratings from nine contemporary LLMs and 779 valid Chinese participant sessions under 3-point category, 7-point Likert, and 0–100 slider formats. The primary outcome was the paired female-minus-male perceived attack-rating gap, ΔF−M\Delta_{F-M}, summarized with Cohen’s dzd_z. Humans and LLMs agreed in direction: all 36 rater-by-scale cells had positive ΔF−M\Delta_{F-M}. The gap was largest in sexualization and appearance domains for both humans and LLMs. Agreement was conditional across stricter comparisons: magnitude depended on the human reference group, human dzd_z was flat or smaller under finer response formats, most LLM dzd_z values were larger, and item-level correspondence remained weak. These findings indicate that LLM ratings can capture broad target-gender patterns in perceived attack ratings, with scale-sensitive and fine-grained structure still differing from human ratings. Human–LLM alignment in this setting should be reported with explicit reference to human subgroup, attack domain, response scale, and analytic level.

We constructed 371 Chinese gender-reversal minimal pairs.

Nine LLMs rated each pair on 3-point, 7-point, and slider scales.

Human raters completed matched 3-point, 7-point, and slider ratings.

Female-targeted versions received higher perceived-attack ratings.

Sexualization, appearance, and response scale shaped human–LLM alignment.

large language models ,gendered attacks ,Chinese ,minimal-pair design ,human–LLM alignment ,rating scales

Introduction

Online hate and harassment undermine individual well-being, public discourse, and platform governance (Schoenebeck et al., 2023). Chinese online hostility requires language-specific evidence because gender antagonism, misogynistic expression, and anti-feminist discourse have become visible forms of online hostility with linguistic forms and platform dynamics that differ from Western corpora (Jiang et al., 2021; Jing-Schmidt & Peng, 2018; Liao, 2024). Although Chinese offensive-language resources have expanded in recent years (Deng et al., 2022; Lu et al., 2023), research specifically on Chinese gendered hate speech remains limited.

At the same time, large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used as scalable raters of offensive and hateful text. For gendered hate-speech evaluation, the central measurement question is not only whether a text contains hostile language, but whether the same hostile content is perceived differently when it targets women rather than men. Existing evidence gives limited guidance on this question because naturally occurring corpora often entangle target gender with wording, severity, topic, and context.

This study examines whether Chinese human raters and contemporary LLMs perceive matched female-targeted and male-targeted versions of the same attack sentence differently. We use a Chinese minimal gender-reversal corpus, human and LLM raters, and three response formats to test whether the female-minus-male perceived-aggressiveness gap appears, where it is concentrated by attack domain, and whether human–LLM alignment depends on human reference group and response scale.

Research Background and Measurement Framework

LLM hate-speech evaluation and human perceptual reference

The empirical and methodological literature on hate speech is much larger in English-language and Western-centered settings, where research and moderation debates have often been organized around protected categories such as race, ethnicity, religion, nationality, sexuality, and gender, and where racialized hate and racial bias in hate-speech detection have been especially visible concerns (Sap et al., 2019; Windisch et al., 2022). This leaves a linguistic and substantive gap for Chinese gendered hostility: current evidence gives limited guidance on how gendered hostility, including hostility toward women, is recognized, interpreted, and perceived as harmful in Chinese online contexts.

LLMs are increasingly proposed as scalable raters for offensive and hateful text in content moderation, toxicity scoring, social-bias evaluation, and computational social-science annotation (He et al., 2024; Ziems et al., 2024). Some studies find that LLMs can approximate human annotations or make context-sensitive hate-speech evaluations that align with human judgement (Davidson, 2026; He et al., 2024). Other evidence shows that LLM judgements can vary across models, prompts, annotation instructions, lexical cues, and the social positions of annotators and targets (Chiu et al., 2022; Giorgi et al., 2025). This mixed evidence is important because hate-speech evaluation depends on how people perceive a text as attacking, harmful, or degrading. Aggressiveness, offensiveness, and discrimination are social-perceptual judgements about how a text is experienced within a language community. In these tasks, people are potential recipients of the attack, and their perceptions define the human reference that model ratings should be compared against.

Minimal gender-reversal design and attack domains

Evaluating when LLM ratings converge with or diverge from human perceptions first requires an appropriate corpus of Chinese gendered attacks. Existing hate-speech corpora are valuable because they show how hostility appears in real online environments: they preserve naturally occurring vocabulary, platform contexts, and the empirical distribution of targets and topics. For the present question, prevalence-oriented corpora raise three problems. First, many widely used resources are English-language datasets, which provide limited coverage of Chinese platform language, gendered slang, and local discursive contexts. Second, even gender-hate resources in English or Chinese are usually natural collections with limited target-gender balancing; female- and male-targeted items may differ in wording, severity, and attack form simply because those differences exist in the source environment. Third, naturally occurring gendered attacks span multiple domains, such as appearance, sexuality, competence, morality, and social roles. This diversity is substantively important, and it means that ratings can vary because of topic and attack type as well as the gendered target. If these factors remain entangled, a higher perceived-aggressiveness rating for one target group could reflect more severe wording, a different attack domain, or a different distribution of topics (Mathew et al., 2021).

To address these limitations, this study uses a Chinese minimal gender-reversal design. In this design, each pair contains two attack sentences that are identical except for the gendered expression referring to the target; the design estimates the female-minus-male rating gap while holding hostile content constant. It addresses the corpus problems outlined above in three ways. First, all items are Chinese attack sentences developed for this study. Second, each female-targeted version is paired with a sentence-template-matched male-targeted version, so wording, attack content, and attack intensity are controlled across target gender. Third, each item is annotated for attack domain, including sexualization, appearance, capability, morality, and social roles. Domain annotation is included because the meaning of a target-gender contrast may differ across forms of attack. Online sexism and misogyny are often realized through sexualized degradation, appearance policing, and gender-role enforcement (Jing-Schmidt & Peng, 2018; Dutta et al., 2025; Fontanella et al., 2024). Social-cognition research also treats person evaluation as multidimensional, including agency, competence, warmth, and morality (Abele & Wojciszke, 2007; Fiske et al., 2007). These literatures support treating attack domain as a design feature for locating where the female–male perceived-aggressiveness gap is most pronounced. Methodologically, this design follows a broader minimal-pair and counterfactual tradition in NLP bias research. Winogender and WinoBias, for example, use sentence pairs or schemas that differ in gendered pronouns or gender-linked references to test whether coreference systems behave differently when the gender cue changes (Rudinger et al., 2018; Zhao et al., 2018). Related counterfactual data augmentation work generates paired texts by swapping gendered words such as he/she or man/woman, with additional grammatical changes only when a language requires agreement preservation (Lu et al., 2018; Zmigrod et al., 2019). The present study applies this logic to Chinese gendered attack sentences.

Human subgroups in gendered hate perception

To evaluate human–LLM alignment in this corpus, the human reference must also be disaggregated. A pooled human average is useful as an overall benchmark, although it can obscure subgroup differences that are substantively central to gendered hate-speech perception. In gender-targeted attacks, female and male raters may experience the same hostile sentence as differentially aggressive, because the text can be closer to one group’s lived vulnerability, social position, or prior exposure to gendered derogation. Subjective annotation research shows that disagreement can reflect meaningful differences in perception (Davani et al., 2022; Larimore et al., 2021), and model outputs may align more closely with some annotator groups than others (Santy et al., 2023; H. Sun et al., 2025). Recent work further shows that individual-level subjective text perceptions are difficult to recover from demographic prompting or a single aggregate baseline (Orlikowski et al., 2025). Assessing LLM alignment in a gender-hate task requires a specified human reference, including female and male raters.

Response scales as measurement conditions

Perceived aggressiveness is measured through a response scale. Scale format can shape human ratings because it defines how much distinction a rater can make and how strongly the task encourages categorical versus graded judgement. Coarse scales, such as binary or very small-category formats, can force respondents toward sharper decisions and may increase threshold-dependent or extreme responding. More fine-grained scales, such as 7-point, visual-analogue, or slider formats, can give respondents more room to express small differences; they may also introduce greater dispersion, more idiosyncratic use of numerical space, or weaker gains once the number of categories exceeds what respondents can use reliably. Psychometric studies show that the number of response options affects reliability, validity, discriminating power, and response distributions in human ratings (Preston & Colman, 2000; Lozano et al., 2008; Simms et al., 2019; Abulela & Khalaf, 2024), and recent comparisons of Likert, visual analogue, and slider formats show that response interfaces can change the resolution and distribution of human judgements (Kloft et al., 2024; Haslbeck et al., 2025; Vollbracht et al., 2026). LLM research has rarely treated the corresponding issue as a measurement question. It remains unclear whether asking an LLM to rate the same content on a 5-point, 7-point, or 0–100 scale merely rescales the output, or whether the response format changes the model’s perceived-severity pattern and its alignment with human raters.

The Present Study

The present study tests Chinese gendered hate perception with 371 minimal pairs of attack-laden sentences. Each pair differs only in the target’s gender referent. Human raters and nine LLMs rated the same materials under 3-point category, 7-point Likert, and 0–100 slider formats. The primary outcome is the paired female-minus-male perceived attack-rating gap, ΔF−M\Delta_{F-M}, summarized with Cohen’s dzd_z across paired items. The study asks three research questions, with RQ1 divided into three target-gender comparison subquestions.

RQ1a. When the same Chinese attack sentence differs only in the target’s gender, do the female-targeted and male-targeted versions receive the same perceived-aggressiveness ratings?

RQ1b. Do human raters and LLMs show the same female–male target-gender pattern?

RQ1c. Do female and male human raters show the same female–male target-gender pattern?

RQ2. Within each attack domain, do female-targeted and male-targeted versions receive the same perceived-aggressiveness ratings, and do humans and LLMs show the same domain-level pattern?

RQ3. Does the female–male target-gender comparison depend on response scale, and do humans and LLMs show the same scale-response pattern across 3-point, 7-point, and slider ratings?

Methods

Human raters

Chinese participants were recruited online through social-media recruitment. After quality-control screening, the analytic sample contained 779 valid rating sessions from 712 unique participants. Each valid session was assigned to one response format and included a pseudo-random subset of approximately 75 sentence versions, randomized item order, and attention checks. Each participant saw only one gender version of any template. Human female- and male-targeted item means were estimated across participants and paired at the template level.

The session-level sample was nearly gender-balanced across scales (Table 1) and concentrated among 18–34-year-old Chinese internet users (Table 2). Item-level means were computed separately within each rater group and scale. Human rater groups were the pooled aggregate, female participants, and male participants; these were compared with the nine LLMs. The participant pool is a nonrepresentative online sample (see Limitations). All experiments received host-institution ethics approval.

Valid rating sessions by participant gender and assigned rating scale. The main human analyses use session as the analysis unit.

LLM raters

Nine contemporary LLMs were queried in November 2025 through the OpenRouter API using an OpenAI-compatible client: Anthropic Claude-Opus-4.5, DeepSeek-R1, DeepSeek-V3.2, Google Gemma-4-31B, Meta Llama-4-Maverick, Moonshot Kimi-K2-Thinking, OpenAI GPT-5.1, Qwen-2.5-72B-Instruct, and Z-AI GLM-4.6. The panel was selected to cover widely used closed- and open-weight model families available through the same inference interface. Each model rated both versions of every minimal pair under all three response formats. The main minimal-pair analyses use zero-shot ratings; temperature was set to 0, and each model returned a structured numeric rating for each item. The analysis treats each production model as a fixed rater and leaves population-level variation across LLMs unestimated.

Materials: minimal gender-reversal corpus

A native Chinese-speaking research team constructed 371 minimal pairs of attack-laden sentences, yielding 742 gender-targeted sentence versions. Within each pair, the two versions were identical except for a gender-referent substitution (e.g., she ↔\leftrightarrow he; woman ↔\leftrightarrow man; female-coded vs. male-coded names or kin terms). This construction holds attack content constant while varying the target’s gender referent.

The item pool used a three-level coding framework derived from prior work on interpersonal derogation, gendered hostility, online sexism, and person evaluation, plus inductive review of Chinese online hostile expressions (Cowan & Hodge, 1996; Abele & Wojciszke, 2007; Fiske et al., 2007; Jing-Schmidt & Peng, 2018; Jiang et al., 2021; Dutta et al., 2025; Fontanella et al., 2024). The first level contained 10 attack domains: sexualization, appearance, gender role/expression, interpersonal relations, moral character, economic resources, social status, emotional stability, capability/competence, and intellectual rationality. The second and third levels guided stimulus construction and are shown in Appendix Table 8 for representative examples.

Candidate expressions were adapted from naturally occurring Chinese online attack language and supplemented with AI-assisted drafting. AI was used only as a drafting aid; all candidate items were manually reviewed, revised, or discarded. Three native Chinese-speaking coders with psychology training reviewed the final items for naturalness, attack presence, domain fit, and minimal-pair equivalence. Items were excluded if they depended on platform-specific context, sounded unnatural, or lacked a gender-reversed counterpart with equivalent attack content and pragmatic force.

Rating task and response scales

The task was to rate the perceived aggressiveness of each sentence. Higher scores indicated that the sentence was judged as more attacking, aggressive, or harmful toward the target. Human and LLM raters used the same three response formats: a 3-point category scale (0=0 = non-attacking, 1=1 = mildly attacking, 2=2 = strongly attacking), a 7-point Likert-type scale from 0 to 6, and a 0–100 slider. These formats operationalize rating granularity from coarse category judgement to finer numerical severity judgement while keeping the rated construct constant.

Procedure

Human participants completed the task online after being assigned to one response scale. Each participant saw only one gender version of any template, which prevented direct comparison of a female-targeted and male-targeted version by the same participant. Item order was randomized, and sentence versions were assigned so that female- and male-targeted versions could be compared at the template level after aggregation.

LLM ratings followed the same item and scale structure. For each model, both gender versions of every template were rated under each response format. The prompt specified the rating scale and requested a structured numeric response; ratings were then parsed and paired by template, target gender, model, and scale before analysis.

Statistical analysis

The primary item-level outcome was the female-minus-male perceived attack-rating gap, ΔF−M(i)=sF(i)−sM(i),\Delta^{(i)}_{F-M} \;=\; s^{(i)}_F - s^{(i)}_M , where sF(i)s^{(i)}_F and sM(i)s^{(i)}_M are the mean perceived attack ratings of the female-targeted and male-targeted versions of item ii within a rater group and scale. The summary effect size for a rater-by-scale cell was Cohen’s dzd_z, dz(r,c)=ΔF−M(i)¯iSD̂(ΔF−M(i))i,d_z(r, c) \;=\; \frac{\overline{\Delta^{(i)}_{F-M}}_i}{\widehat{\mathrm{SD}}\!\left(\Delta^{(i)}_{F-M}\right)_i}, computed across the 371 paired items as a paired standardized effect size (Cohen, 1988). A positive ΔF−M\Delta_{F-M} means that the female-targeted version was rated as more aggressive than the matched male-targeted version. The paired item was the unit of analysis for dzd_z. The full design crossed 12 rater groups (9 LLMs plus 3 human strata) with 3 scales, yielding 36 rater-by-scale cells. For descriptive cross-scale comparisons, item means and gaps were normalized by scale maximum to place the three scales on a common 0–1 axis; dzd_z itself is scale-free.

The analyses followed the reporting order implied by the research questions. RQ1a tested whether ΔF−M\Delta_{F-M} differed from zero when only the target gender changed. RQ1b compared the human and LLM female–male target-gender patterns, with the pooled human aggregate and the female- and male-participant strata treated as distinct references. RQ1c compared female and male human raters directly. For RQ2, we computed ΔF−M\Delta_{F-M} and dzd_z within the 10 first-level attack domains and examined which domains produced the largest female-minus-male gaps. For RQ3, we compared ΔF−M\Delta_{F-M} and dzd_z across the 3-point, 7-point, and slider scales and examined whether the scale-response pattern was parallel for humans and LLMs. Item-level ICC and domain-profile correlations were used as auxiliary alignment diagnostics outside the core research questions.

Results

The Results follow the logic of the research questions without using the question labels as section headings. We first test whether matched female- and male-targeted versions receive the same perceived-aggressiveness ratings. We then compare the female–male pattern between humans and LLMs, and between female and male human raters. The final sections examine whether the target-gender comparison varies across attack domains and response scales.

Female-targeted versions receive higher perceived-aggressiveness ratings

We first ask whether two versions of the same Chinese attack sentence receive equal perceived-aggressiveness ratings when only the target gender changes. The evidence departed consistently from equality in the same direction. Every one of the 36 rater-by-scale cells had positive mean ΔF−M\Delta_{F-M} (Figure 1). Among human raters, the female-targeted version received a higher aggregated perceived-attack rating for 58.2%–74.7% of items, depending on participant group and scale. Among LLMs, 3-point-scale ratings frequently produced identical values for the two versions of the same item pair; the mean gap was positive in every model cell, and all 27 LLM cells reached BH-FDR adjusted q<10−7q < 10^{-7} for ΔF−M>0\Delta_{F-M} > 0.

This first result answers RQ1a directly: the two target-gender versions received different ratings. Female-targeted versions were rated as more aggressive on average. This is a direction-level result; effect-size alignment and participant-gender differences are examined in the next sections.

Human and LLM target-gender patterns share direction with reference-dependent magnitude

We next ask whether humans and LLMs show the same female–male target-gender pattern. At the sign level, they do: both rater types produced positive ΔF−M\Delta_{F-M} values across all response scales. The stricter magnitude comparison was conditional on the human reference group. We use the 3-point format as the primary magnitude benchmark because it most closely resembles applied annotation categories; scale dependence is examined separately in Section 5.5. Throughout, “larger gender-differential effect” refers only to a larger positive dzd_z in perceived attack ratings.

On the 3-point scale, the LLMs occupied the interval between the male- and female-participant references. Their dzd_z values ranged from 0.289 to 0.653. At the lower end, DeepSeek-R1 and DeepSeek-V3.2 both had dz=0.289d_z = 0.289, and GLM-4.6 had dz=0.297d_z = 0.297; these values were above the male-participant reference and well below the pooled and female-participant references. At the upper end, Qwen-2.5-72B (dz=0.553d_z = 0.553), Kimi-K2-Thinking (dz=0.572d_z = 0.572), and GPT-5.1 (dz=0.653d_z = 0.653) approached the pooled and female-participant references. Figure 2 shows the same comparison as model-minus-human gaps, with a separate panel for each human reference.

The reference-group pattern mirrors the male- and female-participant separation. Against male participants, all nine LLM point estimates are larger, and six bootstrap intervals are entirely above zero. Against female participants, no LLM point estimate exceeds the female-participant reference; six intervals are entirely below zero, and Qwen-2.5-72B, Kimi-K2-Thinking, and GPT-5.1 overlap zero. Against the overall human aggregate, five LLMs are clearly lower (DeepSeek-R1, DeepSeek-V3.2, GLM-4.6, Llama-4-Maverick, and Gemma-4-31B), and Claude-Opus-4.5, Qwen-2.5-72B, Kimi-K2-Thinking, and GPT-5.1 overlap the aggregate reference.

The sign of the model-minus-human gap changes with the selected human reference. The same nine models are above male participants, at or below female participants, and mixed against the pooled human estimate. RQ1b has a two-level answer: humans and LLMs share the direction of the female-minus-male pattern, and the apparent magnitude of alignment depends on whether the human reference is the pooled aggregate, female raters, or male raters.

Female and male human raters share direction and differ in strength

Within the human sample, female and male raters shared the same positive direction and differed in magnitude. On the 3-point scale, male participants showed a small female-minus-male gap: the mean male-target rating was 0.573 and the mean female-target rating was 0.602, giving a normalized mean gap of 0.030 and dz=0.219d_z = 0.219. Female participants showed a much larger gap: the corresponding means were 0.552 and 0.642, giving a normalized mean gap of 0.090 and dz=0.655d_z = 0.655. The pooled human aggregate fell between these two references, with means of 0.562 and 0.622, a normalized mean gap of 0.060, and dz=0.621d_z = 0.621.

The same ordering appeared across response formats (Table 3). Male participants had dz=0.219d_z = 0.219, 0.1980.198, and 0.1670.167 on the 3-point, 7-point, and slider scales; female participants had dz=0.655d_z = 0.655, 0.7040.704, and 0.5900.590. Female and male raters both rated female-targeted versions as more aggressive on average, with a larger female-minus-male pattern among female raters. This within-human separation explains why the human reference group matters for the LLM comparison in RQ1b.

Human female-minus-male target-gender effects by participant gender and response scale. Positive dzd_z values indicate higher perceived-aggressiveness ratings for female-targeted versions than for matched male-targeted versions.
Response scale Overall humans dzd_z Female raters dzd_z Male raters dzd_z
3-point 0.621 0.655 0.219
7-point Likert 0.588 0.704 0.198
0–100 slider 0.512 0.590 0.167

Sexualization and appearance show the largest domain-level gaps

At the domain level, we ask whether female-targeted and male-targeted versions are rated as equally aggressive within each attack domain, and whether humans and LLMs show the same domain-level pattern. The domain profile shows uneven departures from equality. Both human ratings and the LLM median profile show the largest perceived-rating gaps in sexualization and appearance. Figure 3 shows first-level domain dzd_z values for the three human strata and for the LLM median, with domains kept in a fixed order. Sexualization and appearance have the largest values in both human and LLM summaries.

On the human side, sexualization and appearance are the two highest-dzd_z domains at every scale for the overall human aggregate: sexualization dz=1.47d_z = 1.47, 1.421.42, and 1.181.18 across the three scales, and appearance dz=0.76d_z = 0.76, 0.800.80, and 0.610.61. Female participants show the same two-domain pattern. Male participants have a smaller overall effect; their largest positive domain effects still fall in sexualization and appearance.

The LLM median also places the largest values in these domains, with some differences in internal ordering. On the 3-point scale, some models flatten or invert the sexualization-above-appearance ordering; for example, DeepSeek-V3.2 has sexualization dz=−0.06d_z = -0.06 and appearance dz=0.37d_z = 0.37. On the 7-point and slider scales, most models show sexualization above appearance. These results place the target-gender gap most clearly in domains tied to gendered bodily evaluation and sexual shaming.

Cohen’s dzd_z in the two largest-gap domains, by rater and scale. The four LLMs are illustrative and span the range of behaviour observed in the full panel. Humans show the sexualization-above-appearance ordering at every scale. Most LLMs lack this internal ordering on the 3-point scale and show it on the 7-point and slider scales.
Sexualization dzd_z Appearance dzd_z
2-4 (lr)5-7 Rater 3pt 7pt Slider 3pt 7pt Slider

Female participants 1.33 1.35 1.09 0.64 0.81 0.69

Response format changes the target-gender comparison

Finally, we ask whether the target-gender comparison depends on response scale, and whether humans and LLMs show the same scale-response pattern. The scale pattern differs between humans and LLMs. Figure 4 shows the cross-scale trajectory of dzd_z. In the present data, human effects stay flat or decline as the scale becomes finer: overall humans move from 0.6210.621 to 0.5880.588 to 0.5120.512, male participants from 0.2190.219 to 0.1980.198 to 0.1670.167, and female participants from 0.6550.655 to 0.7040.704 to 0.5900.590. Most LLMs move in the opposite direction. Six of nine models show monotonic or near-monotonic increases, with Gemma-4-31B rising from 0.4080.408 to 0.7730.773 to 1.0161.016.

This scale dependence changes the descriptive human–LLM comparison. On the 3-point scale, five of nine LLMs sit below the overall-human reference. On the slider, five of nine sit at or above it. The coarse annotation categories place more models below the overall-human reference than the slider does. This contrast is treated as a measurement-level pattern, without inference over a population of models.

Cohen’s dzd_z of ΔF−M\Delta_{F-M} as a function of rating granularity. On the human side, dzd_z stays flat or decreases as the scale becomes finer; on the LLM side, most dzd_z values increase. The same human–LLM comparison is different on the 3-point scale than on the slider. The cross-rater scale-response contrast is descriptive in the present analysis.
Rater 3pt dzd_z 7pt dzd_z Slider dzd_z Trend across scales

Llama-4-Maverick 0.379 0.477 0.409 7pt peak
Qwen-2.5-72B 0.553 0.578 0.686 increase

A human-side repeated-measures ANOVA clarifies the human component of this pattern. In a stimulus-as-unit analysis of normalized ΔF−M\Delta_{F-M}, participant gender had a substantially larger effect, F(1,370)=111.17F(1, 370) = 111.17, p<.001p < .001, ηG2=.044\eta^2_G = .044, than scale, F(2,740)=9.44F(2, 740) = 9.44, Greenhouse–Geisser corrected p<.001p < .001, ηG2=.007\eta^2_G = .007. The participant-gender by scale interaction was not significant, F(2,740)=1.79F(2, 740) = 1.79, p=.169p = .169. Female participants exceeded male participants within every scale after BH-FDR correction. Participant gender is the larger human-side determinant of ΔF−M\Delta_{F-M}; scale remains a smaller detectable measurement condition. The cross-rater human–LLM scale contrast remains descriptive because LLMs are deterministic raters and provide no participant-level random effects.

Because RQ1b and RQ3 both concern human–LLM alignment, we also checked whether aggregate similarity implies finer-grained interchangeability. The auxiliary indicators correspond to distinct alignment claims. At the aggregate level, similarity means a small absolute gap between a model’s overall dzd_z and a human reference group’s overall dzd_z. At the domain level, similarity means a high Spearman correlation between the model and human vectors over the 10 first-level attack domains. Item-level ICC(2,1) asks whether a model reproduces the human ordering of the 371 individual sentence pairs.

The levels produce different closest-model rankings (Figure 5; Table 6). At the aggregate level, several models nearly match a chosen human reference: the smallest absolute dzd_z gaps are 0.032, 0.010, and 0.002 against overall humans across the three scales, and 0.002–0.012 against female participants. At the domain level, the best 10-domain Spearman correlations range from 0.56 to 0.85 across the nine human-reference-by-scale cells. The domain count is small (k=10k=10), and Spearman correlations have a wide chance band: a two-sided permutation null with two random rankings of 10 items has an approximate 95%95\% interval of [−0.65,+0.65][-0.65, +0.65]. Under this null, |ρ||\rho| must exceed about 0.650.65 to be distinguishable from random ranking. Per-cell two-sided permutation pp values are reported in Table 6 and computed using 10510^5 random permutations. Seven of the nine best-model cells reach p<0.05p<0.05; the remaining two are both anchored on the female-participant reference, where the best-model ρ\rho values are 0.56 (3-point, p=0.10p=0.10) and 0.64 (7-point, p=0.06p=0.06). The female-participant slider cell exceeds chance (ρ=0.85\rho=0.85, p=0.003p=0.003). These permutation values provide a chance-ranking baseline for each observed domain-profile correlation; they are unadjusted for model selection across the nine-model panel. The domain-level result echoes the shared sexualization and appearance pattern in Section 5.4 for most reference-by-scale cells. The female-anchored coarse-scale cells remain within the chance band given the available domain count.

At the item level, the best ICC(2,1) in each cell remains low, ranging from 0.089 to 0.222. Against overall humans, the best item-level ICC rises from 0.089 on the 3-point scale to 0.158 on the 7-point scale and 0.222 on the slider, with Claude-Opus-4.5 the closest model on the two finer scales. The item-level closest model also varies across human-reference-by-scale cells.

Closest model by analysis level. Values in the aggregate column are absolute dzd_z gaps, where smaller is closer. Values in the domain column are Spearman ρ\rho over the 10 first-level attack domains, with the two-sided permutation pp value (105^{5} random rank-pair permutations) for each best-model ρ\rho given alongside. Values in the item column are ICC(2,1) over the 371 paired items.
Human reference Scale Closest aggregate dzd_z Closest 10-domain profile (ρ\rho, pp) Closest item profile
Overall humans 3-point GPT-5.1 (.032) Gemma-4-31B (.66, p=.044p=.044) Llama-4-Maverick (.089)
Overall humans 7-point Qwen-2.5-72B (.010) Claude-Opus-4.5 (.66, p=.044p=.044) Claude-Opus-4.5 (.158)
Overall humans Slider DeepSeek-R1 (.002) Kimi-K2-Thinking (.76, p=.016p=.016) Claude-Opus-4.5 (.222)
Female participants 3-point GPT-5.1 (.002) Gemma-4-31B (.56, p=.097p=.097) DeepSeek-R1 (.100)
Female participants 7-point Claude-Opus-4.5 (.012) Gemma-4-31B (.64, p=.055p=.055) Claude-Opus-4.5 (.116)
Female participants Slider Kimi-K2-Thinking (.003) DeepSeek-V3.2 (.85, p=.003p=.003) Qwen-2.5-72B (.112)
Male participants 3-point DeepSeek-V3.2 (.070) Qwen-2.5-72B (.72, p=.024p=.024) Llama-4-Maverick (.096)
Male participants 7-point GLM-4.6 (.155) DeepSeek-V3.2 (.81, p=.007p=.007) GLM-4.6 (.155)
Male participants Slider Llama-4-Maverick (.242) Kimi-K2-Thinking (.73, p=.021p=.021) DeepSeek-R1 (.174)

Which model is closest depends on the level of analysis. GPT-5.1 and Qwen-2.5-72B can be closest in aggregate magnitude, Gemma-4-31B or Kimi-K2-Thinking can be closest in the 10-domain profile, and Claude-Opus-4.5 is often closest at the item level for overall humans on finer scales. These rankings describe different alignment claims and should be reported separately. The overall result is summarized in Table 7.

Results organized by the stated research-question order. Auxiliary item-level and domain-profile diagnostics support the scale and alignment interpretation and remain outside the core research questions.
Result focus Answer Main evidence Result summary
Target-gender equality No 36/36 cells have positive ΔF−M\Delta_{F-M} Female-targeted versions are rated higher
Human–LLM pattern Direction yes; magnitude conditional Models are above male raters, at or below female raters, and mixed against pooled humans Alignment depends on the human reference
Human rater gender Direction yes; strength differs Female-rater dzd_z exceeds male-rater dzd_z on all scales Female raters show a larger gap
Domain pattern Yes Sexualization and appearance are the shared largest-gap domains Gap concentrates in gendered attack domains
Scale pattern Yes Human dzd_z decreases or peaks; most LLM dzd_z values increase; best item-level ICC is 0.222 Response format changes the comparison

Discussion

The same attack is perceived as more aggressive when it targets women

The central finding is that target gender systematically changed perceived aggressiveness even when attack content was held constant. Across all human and LLM rater-by-scale cells, female-targeted versions received higher perceived-attack ratings than matched male-targeted versions. The result concerns perceived severity in Chinese gendered attack language: the same hostile sentence received different ratings when the target was female versus male.

The size of this effect depends on the human reference group. Female raters showed a larger female-minus-male gap than male raters, with the pooled human estimate falling between them. LLMs mapped differently onto different human baselines: on the 3-point scale, they were above the male-rater reference, at or below the female-rater reference, and mixed relative to the pooled human reference. Claims about LLM alignment in this setting require an explicit human reference group.

Sexualization and appearance are the key attack domains

The target-gender gap was unevenly distributed across attack content. For both humans and LLMs, the largest female-minus-male gaps appeared in sexualization and appearance. These domains directly invoke gendered bodily evaluation, sexual moral judgement, and appearance-based social scrutiny. The additional perceived aggressiveness of female-targeted versions is concentrated in domains where Chinese gender hostility is especially tied to bodies, sexuality, and gender norms.

This domain pattern also clarifies the observed human–LLM similarity. LLMs produced a positive average gap and placed much of the gap in the same broad content regions as human raters. Domain-profile correlations and item-level ICCs show limits to this similarity. The models captured the broad location of the effect more reliably than the item-by-item ordering of which specific sentences produced the largest gaps.

Response format changes the human–LLM comparison

The scale results show that response format is part of the measurement design. In the present data, human dzd_z values stayed flat or became smaller as the scale moved from 3-point to 7-point to slider. Most LLM dzd_z values became larger. This pattern changes the descriptive alignment conclusion: the same model panel can look more moderate than the pooled human reference on a coarse 3-point scale and closer to, or above, that reference on the slider.

This finding matters because LLM evaluation studies often treat the response scale as a prompt-design choice. Here, response format changed the substantive comparison between human and model judgements. A model that appears aligned under one scale may differ under another. Evaluations of LLM social judgements should report response format as part of the measurement design.

Implications

The theoretical contribution is to frame Chinese gendered hate evaluation as a target-gender perception problem. The minimal-pair design shows that the same hostile content can be perceived differently when the target gender changes, with differences structured by attack domain and response scale. The methodological contribution is to specify human–LLM alignment as a profile: direction, effect size, human reference group, domain structure, response scale, and item-level correspondence can lead to different interpretations.

For platform governance and model evaluation, the findings caution against reliance on a single pooled-human label. Pooled-human aggregates hide subgroup differences, aggregate effect sizes leave item-level interchangeability unresolved, and response scales can change the human–LLM comparison. If the goal is to simulate human judgement, the human reference group and scale must be named. If the goal is content moderation or harm screening, closeness to a pooled human average requires an explicit policy standard for what kinds of gendered harm should be prioritized.

Limitations and Future Work

The minimal-pair items are Chinese. Whether the same pattern of measurement-conditional alignment holds in a parallel English corpus, where gender-marking morphology and pronoun systems differ, is an open question for follow-up work. The participant pool is a nonrepresentative Chinese online sample with a young, urban skew; the human reference values reported here should be read as the values of this pool. The LLM panel is a snapshot of November 2025 production models; reproducibility under later versions and under fine-tuned annotation prompts will require re-evaluation. We used zero-shot prompting as the main setting, leaving chain-of-thought, system-prompt scaffolding, and task-specific fine-tuning for future work. The minimal-pair design isolates the gender referent and may omit pragmatic and contextual features of real hate speech, including multimodal cues, reply context, and audience-targeted dog-whistle language. Participant gender is treated as binary in the present analysis because the data permit only that contrast at sample sizes large enough for stable per-cell dzd_z; future work with larger non-binary samples should examine more nuanced identity variables. Finally, the three rating formats may differ in granularity, cognitive demand, time on task, and anchor availability; disentangling those mechanisms will require dedicated psychophysical follow-up.

Conclusion

When attack content is held fixed, humans and LLMs agree on the direction of the gender-differential perceived-rating effect: female-targeted versions are rated as more attacking on average. Their perceived-rating gaps are also largest in sexualization and appearance. Full interchangeability is unsupported by these data. Magnitude depends on the human reference group, scale changes the model-human comparison, and item-level ICC remains low even when aggregate dzd_z is close. LLM alignment on gender-differential attack ratings should be reported as a profile across reference groups, response formats, and analytic levels. Evaluations should specify whether the goal is to reproduce a particular human reference, apply an external fairness criterion, or measure variation in human perception.

Representative minimal-pair stimuli

Table 8 shows one representative item from each first-level attack domain, with two additional high-intensity examples from the appearance and sexualization domains. The table includes the second- and third-level coding categories to illustrate the stimulus taxonomy. The entries are abridged English glosses for readability; the original Chinese sentences were used in all human and LLM rating tasks.

Representative minimal-pair stimuli by first-level attack domain, with two additional high-intensity examples from the two largest-gap domains. Numbers in parentheses give the number of templates in the 371-item stimulus set for that domain. English glosses are provided for readability; all rating tasks used the original Chinese sentences.
First-level domain Second-level attribute Third-level strategy Female-targeted gloss Male-targeted gloss
Appearance (37) Physical appearance Ugly / unattractive This woman is so ugly that even she would scare herself in a dream. This man is so ugly that even he would scare himself in a dream.
Appearance Physical appearance Ugly / unattractive This woman looks so repulsive that people would lose their appetite just seeing her. This man looks so repulsive that people would lose their appetite just seeing him.
Capability / competence (47) Work ability Low efficiency / procrastination This woman works painfully slowly; even a simple project drags on forever. This man works painfully slowly; even a simple project drags on forever.
Intellectual rationality (40) Intelligence Stupid / low intelligence This woman is hopelessly stupid, with a brain like paste. This man is hopelessly stupid, with a brain like paste.
Emotional stability (38) Emotionality label Too emotional / irrational This woman gets carried away and starts attacking before knowing the facts. This man gets carried away and starts attacking before knowing the facts.
Moral character (52) Honesty Hypocritical / two-faced This woman smiles to your face and betrays you behind your back. This man smiles to your face and betrays you behind your back.
Sexualization / sexual shaming (33) Sexual morality shaming Promiscuity / poor sexual conduct This woman’s private life is a mess; she looks dirty. This man’s private life is a mess; he looks dirty.
Sexualization / sexual shaming Sexual morality shaming Promiscuity / poor sexual conduct This woman sleeps around and still pretends to be respectable; she looks filthy. This man sleeps around and still pretends to be respectable; he looks filthy.
Interpersonal relations (40) Social ability Disliked / not accepted No one planned to include this woman; forcing herself in only makes her look unwanted. No one planned to include this man; forcing himself in only makes him look unwanted.
Social status (36) Legitimacy of authority Too junior / unqualified This woman has only been around for a few days and now wants to lecture others. Who gave her the right? This man has only been around for a few days and now wants to lecture others. Who gave him the right?
Economic resources (34) Income ability Poor earning capacity This kind of woman lives on a tiny salary and deserves to stay poor forever. This kind of man lives on a tiny salary and deserves to stay poor forever.
Gender role / expression (14) Gender expression Insufficient femininity / masculinity This woman has no femininity at all; what kind of woman is she? This man has no masculinity at all; what kind of man is he?

Data and Code

All artifacts referenced here, including the revised reference-group bootstrap plot, domain forest plot, scale-response trajectory, item-level dissociation figure, analysis tables, and source code, are available in the project repository.
